title: Gaby Brogan -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Gaby Brogan -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: You're muted.
Thanks.
Hey, how are you?
Lovely to meet you.
You too.
How's it going?
Yeah, good.
Thank you.
How are you?
Good.
So I'm a little bit late.
Where are you based?
So I am based at the moment in curiosity.
So just working remotely from the Caribbean, but usually I'm back and forth kind of based in the UK, working remotely on this side of the world.
Nice.
Yeah, that's amazing.
I have never been to Curacao, but I grew up in the Caribbean, so very jealous that you're there now.
Oh, awesome.
Whereabouts were you?
St.
Thomas, so kind of all the way up the arc, but.
Very cool.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, we can jump in since I was a little bit late.
Standard sort of interview format.
I'll give you a little bit of perspective on how I'm thinking about the role.
We can talk about you and then I'll leave like 10-ish minutes at the end for you to ask me any questions that you have.
Perfect.
So GrowthX at its heart, we are, we operate as a services marketing sort of agency model and we are delivering.
Delivering organic SEO content, really, and geo, like really planning for AI.
So we're not doing like social or video or anything like that, really thinking about like blog content and building out like written content for companies.
And while we operate in an agency model, we do everything that we're building comes from a platform that we have internally.
Our founding engineer built it out.
It's called Atlas.
Eventually, we will be offering that like it will be client facing, but probably not for a very long time.
But it is like sort of we're generating content using AI for clients and managing editors are kind of the central part of the pods in the delivery org, the client facing org that I lead.
So managing editors are project managers, thinking about week on week.
Are we tracking against our scope of work?
Usually it's around five articles a week that we're delivering for clients.
Might be some programmatic SEO work, things like that.
So are we meeting that expectation?

And then they're working with content managers who are the, that's the team that's kind of in the platform day-to-day, running the AI pipelines, generating that first draft, and then sharing that with the managing editors to edit content.
Managing editors are editing three to five pieces of content a week, or per client per, sorry, five pieces of content per week for three to five clients a week.
So it's around 15 to 25 pieces of content.
And a week that you're editing.
And then, and delivering for clients.
And then the other piece of it there, so there is a client facing aspect.

And then because we're built off of that internal, like everything that we do is built off of that AI platform, understanding kind of what makes content good, that editorial mindset, being able to translate that into feedback for our engineering team about how we can improve the workflows, kind of what's missing, things like that is going to be really useful.
So like some, like AI experience, and the ability to kind of translate what works in content into, like, sort of break it down into like the technical pieces of it is really useful.
That was a little bit rambling, so sorry, but hopefully you got, you got a sense of it.
Do you have any like questions before we jump?
No, that kind of that workflow sort of makes sense to me.
think it was a good overview.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, why don't we talk about you then?
Perfect.
So I guess just, yeah.
Oh, sorry.
Go ahead.
Yeah.
I was going to say, why don't you give me your like elevator pitch background?
Yeah, of course.
So I kind of started my career about 10 years ago working at Accenture.

So in management consulting, and that kind of gave me a really strong technical grounding in sort of project management and project delivery across Fortune 500 clients, which was a really solid foundation for then the next phase of my career where I went and joined a little ad tech startup, so doing programmatic advertising and just sort of getting into that side of marketing because I always knew I was interested in that, but had obviously been doing more high level project management and project.
And consulting projects at Accenture.
And I wanted to kind of merge those skill sets to bring in the creative and the marketing side.
So after a year or two of that, I started my own marketing consulting company.
So I've been freelancing for the last seven years, six, seven years.
And it's been a fantastic experience learning to obviously run my own business, get my own clients, take them through the entire workflow.
So, you know, first, the first kind of meetings with them through to delivering everything from kind of, you mentioned, obviously, blogs, landing pages, that content driven stuff.
But also I've ghostwritten books.
So kind of a real, a real range of projects that I've been able to apply my kind of marketing and project management skills to.
And at the moment, most of my time is spent kind of between two international marketing agencies.
So I'm part time with both of them as a freelancer.
But for one, I am head of content.
So I run the.

Entire content team, which, I mean, it's been five years that I've been in that role with them, and that's called King's Crest Global, the company, and on and off we've had kind of writers coming in to work under me, at other times it's just been me doing things, me and the AI together, so that's been a real learning experience, kind of managing people through to managing AI in the last few years.

And then the other half of my time, I'm working at Culture Kids, which is another international marketing agency, and there I work with their kind of strategic head of marketing, doing a lot of voice and brand work, focused on a Gen Z audience, that's really like hyper-specific towards one audience, whereas King's Crest Global, our work is international and across industries, so everything from software as a service clients through to like B2B, B2C, direct, companies, so there's a real like range that I've kind of been building up over the last last year.
Yeah.
Seven years in the marketing world.
You've got a lot in the air.
Tell me a little bit about how you, like, organize your time and, like, you know, across those, like, head of content feels like a big job.
So talk to me a little bit more about, like, how you're splitting your time and managing those projects.
Sure.
So I think organization is just, like, absolutely key.
My friends all call me, like, a type A hippie because I'm, like, creative, but very type A.
And so I've got my list.
I've got my tools I use.
I use monday.com.
I use Notion.
And then I also have just, like, well, it kind of depends on the client.
If I have, sometimes I do take on other client projects on top of these two ongoing roles.
So I'm dynamic and flexible with it, but I do have a core base of, like, monday.com to track deliverables.
And that really is how I do it.
Essentially, a lot of communication and a lot of deliverables tracking and project management tools being used to help me do that.
In my head of content role, we do have a project management team that I liaise with on a weekly basis.
I'm running the project management for the clients that are assigned to me, where it is purely content work we're doing.
But this agency does a lot of other work as well, like website design and development.
So there'll be projects where I'm working cross-functionally with the web dev team, with like PPC, you know, it really depends on the project.
But all of that is PracticMonday.com and just aligned with deadlines.
And we're obviously all tagging each other, keeping up to date on Slack and just really communicating.
And, you know, there are enough hours in the day and it's something I really enjoy is having that variety.
And because I think I enjoy it, I don't find it stressful because I'm just like, well, this is fun.
This is what I enjoy in life is having that variety.
that variety.
I never, I never kind of get to a point where I have two huge client deadlines for the two different companies coming up that I haven't pre-planned for.
And, you know, I do work some extra hours, some evenings, and then, you know, with the ebb and flow of agency life, there will then be a slow week afterwards, and that's okay.
And I plan for it, and I just communicate and make sure that we're all on the same page, me and the project managers, as well as, like, the design and dev teams across both agencies.
Awesome.
Yeah.
I mean, it sounds like you've worked it out.
I'm impressed.
So you mentioned a little bit that you've, like, now it's, like, managing AI for writing and things like that.
So talk to me about what that looks like, how you're interacting with it day to day.
Let's start there.
Yeah, so in my head of content role, I'm quite lucky to have a lot of responsibility in terms of how I want to run the...
Mmm.
The area that we're working on.
So essentially, I was able to, from the beginning, start integrating at first ChatGPT, and Gemini, and then kind of like Perplexity, and then finally Jasper into our workflow.
And so the way I use it is to kind of automate blogs, because we have a lot of ongoing blog clients, who are just doing a kind of weekly, weekly or monthly kind of deliveries for an SEO content.
And that's again, across like, such a huge range of spaces, like medical, fashion, software as service.
So that's been interesting to kind of try to automate that blog element.
And I found the task that the previous kind of exercise for this role was to do the task where I was making a brief, right, for the, for eventually, content writers and AI.
And that's really similar to kind of what I do day to day at KC.

At King's Crest, in terms of defining the parameters of what needs to be done, and kind of putting up guardrails around that, sources we're pulling from, how much time we want to spend on it, how many edits we want, rounds of edits we want to do, kind of optimizing for that.

So that's kind of on the SEO content generation, but I'm also using it for the more creative direction pieces that I'm working on, so I'm really enjoying using it as a way to get better ideas at scale for brand voice work.
It's not a big part of what I do at KCG as well, it's like defining brands for our clients, like that's normally phase one of our projects.
And just being able to take what I love about that, which is the creative side, but use it as a brainstorming tool, but also like an ideas generation tool, that's been a big part of the process too.
So yeah, across projects, I've sort of been integrating the AI into every piece of the workflow, so that it's idea generation.
Content generation, and then editing and proofreading, but all with that human eye, to just make sure that it's got essentially my creativity at the moment at the heart of it.
Because I'm the one also on client calls discussing their needs with them.
Yeah.
So it's about also translating their needs to these tools.
Yeah.
Where do you feel like it really, like, shines, and where do you feel like it really fails in that, like, creativity piece?
So where I feel like it really shines is when I have taken the time to really come through with my own unique ideas and bring those to the table.
Because then I think you set the bar for what you're looking for, which is not sort of generic or lowest common denominator content.
You're looking for something that feels more real.
So that's when I find that it helps enhance what's already there.
And also just from an efficiency standpoint, I mean, the amount of blogs that we're obviously generating now is so much higher than what I could do, even with a team of three writers at one
So that's where I think it shines.
Where I think it fails is the editing process.
So I think that the amounts of edits needed at the moment are higher than I'd like them to be.

I do spend a lot of time tweaking and revising to make sure that, you know, you can load up your tone of voice into Jasper, but sometimes it's still going to slip up and have those like the M dashes, or it's going to have a really like, just an obvious tell that this was written by an LLM.
And so I would say I'm spending more time than I'd like on the edits, because I'm trying to obviously get it to a point where I feel confident shipping this off and I can, you know, back it.
And I just think that it's obviously about training the tools, but sometimes it does just slip into, you know, these generic tells and I'm sort of battling that on a day-to-day basis.
Yeah, yeah, that makes sense.
I would that actually like, answers another question that I had, which was like, how much like real uh, uh,
What editing are you doing on a day-to-day basis, just like with the, given the project management and the head of content stuff?
So you're like in content itself on a day-to-day basis too these days.
Yeah, yeah, because I don't have any writers at the moment.
And that's, I, we've, you know, like, I like having a team of writers as well.
I do find it really, I do find it really rewarding to work with a team.
So we've had that before.
And I've also been able to work with those writers with, with the AI too.
So we've kind of had some human oversight the whole time, but at the moment I am doing most of it because we had one girl leave, another girl's gone on maternity leave.
And then, yeah, so then the team is changing.
But it's kind of always been that I'm overseeing the content.
Yeah.
And I started off in this role writing the content from scratch before, before AI.
So yeah, it's been the whole process really.
Yeah.
Cool.
So I think my last question is like, what excites you or interests you about this role at growthx?
Because it seems like you've got like some really exciting things going on.
So just curious about like what you're looking for next and why growthx?
Yeah, so I'm looking to grow and kind of go beyond where my career currently is.
So the startups, the two startups, they're both startups, the marketing agencies I'm with.
And the reason that I am part time is because they do have smaller client bases, they do have smaller kind of runways.
And I'm just looking to kind of grow somewhere that has that potential for me to kind of build my career, but also where I actually really align with the growth of their long term.

vision and just the amount of innovation that even I was able to discuss with Matthew on my last interview was just so inspirational for me and kind of aligned more with my Accenture days where there's that like hunger for
Yeah, like kind of reaching that next level, creating something new.
And I think that growthx has that where agency life perhaps hasn't had that for me.
And I'm very competitive as well as a person.
I really enjoy.
I enjoy learning and growing and I'm competitive across life.
Like I love running.
I'm doing like marathon training.
And I just have felt that where my career is now with my agency life, I'm very comfortable.
And I would love a new challenge to sink my teeth into.
And a challenge that's also at the forefront of something that's reshaping our industry.
I don't want to feel on the back foot.
I want to feel like part of it.
And I feel like I'm bringing that, especially to King's Crest.
But there's only so much when you're just one person who has that kind of mindset or one of a few people with that mindset.
Whereas it feels to me that growthx, there's this alignment on mindset around innovation, AI.
And, you know, even what you've mentioned about, like, GEO, AEO.
So, absolutely right.
thank
Being able to really get to that point already with clients is something that I'm super interested in that we've not been able to get to yet with clients in my current agencies.
Cool.
Well, we have 11 minutes left.
So what questions do you have for me?
Yeah, so I'd love to just hear a bit more about kind of your vision for your kind of area of the business, how you see it growing, how you see it going in the future.
Yeah, so I think, like, we literally have a meeting in an hour to talk about, like, scaling, because demand is not the problem at GrowthX.
It's actually, like, making sure that we're delivering high-quality work.
And, yes, we are an agency model, but the whole idea behind GrowthX is sort of building on the capabilities of AI to, like, create leverage.
And right now we're growing very linearly in terms of, like, adding...
Clients adding people like we can't hire fast enough, which is really exciting.
But I think like, this is a cool time to think about joining the two because it will be tricky as we as we build that leverage.
But also like, I think the expansion opportunities in terms of like, growing teams and managing like larger books of businesses is will be really cool.
Um, so it like, I don't know, I think that answers part of your question.

But, uh, yeah, I think like the way that I'm thinking about the team in the next six months is, um, really excited about where the product is going and what that can unlock, um, in terms of, uh, the team being able to like, think more strategically rather than like, you know, a day to day to day that I kind of described to you, which is a little bit of what's happening today, but more like uh, uh, uh, uh, uh, uh
Being able to read trends and a lot of what we're doing and a lot of what our clients are interested in is around AI visibility and things like that.
And our product team is thinking a lot about how we integrate those sorts of insights into the platform itself.
So I think the way that we serve our clients, both from an org structure and from the kinds of questions that we're able to answer on a week-to-week basis is evolving.
And right now, I feel like I'm constantly behind on it.
But if we can make it work, it's going to be really, really cool.
Awesome.
That's so cool.
And I think there's a lot of conversation happening around that and thinking happening around that, too.
Yes, yes.
So, I mean, I will say it's like a scrappy startup right now.
And, like, it's not always as, like, beautiful as we imagine it to be.
But it is, like, I just invest...
Users are excited, clients are excited, like there's a lot of like, goodwill and interest in what we're doing.
So there's like, we have the high ground.
Very cool.
Yeah, love that kind of like the enthusiasm and energy behind it.
I think that's so key.
Yeah.
Awesome.

That kind of, well, kind of leads me into my next question around like, something that seems really unique to me about growthx, that I've sort of understood so far as this kind of really international team that you're building.
And obviously, it's, well, from what I understand, it's headquartered in California, right?
Yeah.
So, you know, that could also just be that the team would be then from the US mostly.
So what's the philosophy behind this kind of international team that you're building?
Well, so both of our co-founders are not from the US.
So Marcel is Brazilian, and I actually don't know where Daniel's from.
Maybe I should know.
But like, I think that is.
The recognition and acknowledgement that like, there are teams that can do good work all over the world.
And it's a very like, American centric way of thinking about like, the hiring pool to just look for like, talent in the US.
And I mean, everybody that we've hired, it's not like we're hiring people and being like, we're going to train you on this thing.
Like, it's like, they have experience in AI, they have experience in like, content generation.
And so I don't think it's like, we're not unlocking a like, first ever secret.
But this is the first time that I've, I have worked with such an international team, like truly global.
But everybody, you know, I think like, the spirits of the spirit of like, this sucks, it's kind of a weird thing to say.
But like, everybody understands how corporate, the corporate world works, you know, so it's not like,
And there's not a lot of like adjustment in that sense either, you know, it's like, it runs very smoothly, considering, considering how all over the world everybody is.
That's really cool.
Yeah, I love to see that.
Like, I've been working remotely for like six years.
So I've been really asynchronous with my work and working on these really global teams.
That's why I was kind of excited to see it.
But I was really interested to hear the philosophy behind it.
So that's awesome.
And then sort of next question on my list is, like, for you, what would be then the measure of success for a managing editor?
Yeah, so I think what we're looking for is somebody that that can like is forward thinking about problem solving.
And I touched a little bit on this in the way that I in like talking about the platform.
And being able to kind of translate like what makes good into like helping the engineering team understand like how to tweak the prompts and workflows on the back end.

But that sort of like interest and curiosity about like, the inputs that are going into these workflows, and the willingness to dive in and talk about like, what will make them better, rather than being like, I'm going to pull it out of AI and manually fix it and take three hours.
Like, we're going to get leveraged by making the product better.
And that's how we're going to get ahead of the market too.
And so somebody that like really wants, you said you're competitive, that's great.
Like somebody that wants to win in all of those senses, like that's how we're going to win.
And so, you know, not just like, get it done and put it away and like, get it done however you can.
But try and make, like, the process better every time you're going through it.
I mean, that's what makes life easier down the line, too.
But so I think, like, that's a huge measure of success.
We don't have, like, formal KPIs right now of, like, X number of articles.
Like, that's in the SoW.
So, like, are we meeting SoW terms, right?
Like, it's not, I'm not, nobody's tracking, like, laptop time or, like, when you're in the office, when you're online or, like, you do have to overlap with California.
But, like, nobody's saying, like, oh, you have to be working this number of hours or editing or, you know.
We're not strict about the what or the how.
Like, it's, like, we want retention and we want, like, client growth.
And so, like, if you are able to...
Deliver to the contract expectations and like clients are happy, we're happy.
And then like above and beyond is like, are you engaged in the process of making the product and the process across the board better?
Okay, awesome.
That makes sense.
Cool.
It sounds like, again, just a really good, in terms of the retention and everything, like there's been a lot of thought into that and kind of creating that space that's really productive.
Um, so that's cool to, yeah, really cool to hear about.
And I think that was, yeah, that was my last question for you.
Okay, awesome.
Uh, well, Gaby, it was great to meet you.
I'm really, uh, I really enjoyed the conversation.
Um, so, you know, I think the next step is to like a second exercise.
So I'm sure you'll chat to Marilia about that shortly.
Perfect.
That sounds great.
Thank you for taking the time.
Of course.
Have a good one.
Bye.
Thanks, you too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=148.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX operates as a services marketing agency, focusing on AI-driven organic SEO content creation](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=167.0)
  - [Managing Editors are central to client-facing delivery, handling 15-25 pieces of content weekly across 3-5 clients](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=223.0)
  - [Gaby has extensive experience in marketing, content creation, and AI integration across multiple agencies](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=369.0)
  - [GrowthX is experiencing rapid growth and seeking to scale efficiently while maintaining high-quality work](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=1159.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Role Overview

  - [Services marketing agency model delivering organic SEO content](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=167.0)
  - [Focus on AI-driven content creation using internal platform (Atlas)](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=167.0)
  - [Managing Editors:](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=223.0)
      - [Central to client-facing delivery org](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=223.0)
      - [Project manage 3-5 clients, \~5 articles/week each](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=240.0)
      - [Edit 15-25 pieces of content weekly](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=283.0)
      - [Interface with content managers using AI pipelines](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=263.0)
      - [Provide feedback to engineering team on AI improvements](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=306.0)

### Candidate Background (Gaby Brogan)

  - [10 years experience: Accenture (management consulting) → ad tech startup → freelance marketing consultant (7 years)](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=381.0)
  - [Current roles:](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=467.0)
      - [Head of Content at King's Crest Global (5 years)](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=467.0)
          - [Manages entire content team](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=467.0)
          - [Integrated AI tools (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Jasper) into workflow](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=712.0)
      - [Strategic marketing at Culture Kids (Gen Z focus)](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=505.0)
  - [Strong project management and organizational skills](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
  - [Extensive experience with AI content generation and editing](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=698.0)

### AI Integration in Content Creation

  - [Gaby's experience:](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=698.0)
      - [Automated blog creation across diverse industries](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=737.0)
      - [Uses AI for creative direction and brand voice work](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=796.0)
      - [Integrated AI into idea generation, content creation, editing](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=832.0)
  - [AI strengths: Efficiency, idea enhancement when given unique input](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=860.0)
  - [AI weaknesses: Editing process still requires significant human oversight](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=904.0)

### GrowthX Growth and Vision

  - [Rapid client demand, focus on maintaining high-quality work](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=1159.0)
  - [Aiming to create leverage through AI capabilities](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=1159.0)
  - [Product development to integrate AI visibility insights](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=1232.0)
  - [Goal: Enable team to think more strategically and identify trends](https://fathom.video/share/E93pCE7YQJ_qFQeQRi4ChKD5o8rQMzPs?tab=summary&timestamp=1232.0)

### Team Structure and Philosophy
